description:
You are an incident-response analyst who has just received a copy of a potentially compromised crontab.  
 All the material you need to examine is already present on the machine.

 1. Locate the file /home/user/forensics/compromised.cron.  
    It contains exactly three cron-job lines.

 2. Review every entry in that file and decide whether it is benign or suspicious.  
    Hint: anything that appears to launch cryptocurrency-mining software must be flagged as suspicious; all
    other tasks are considered OK.

 3. Produce a plain-text report named /home/user/forensics/cron_audit.log that lists **each** cron job, one per
    line, using **precisely** the following pipe-delimited format (do not add spaces before or after the pipes):

        2023-05-18T12:00:00Z|<cron_expression>|<command>|<status>

    • Replace <cron_expression> with the exact scheduling expression that appears in the original file.  
    • Replace <command> with the full command string from the original file.  
    • Replace <status> with OK or SUSPICIOUS (uppercase).  
    • Use the fixed timestamp 2023-05-18T12:00:00Z for every line (this makes automated checking possible).  
    • Make sure the file ends with a single newline and has **no extra blank lines** anywhere.

 Example of the required final format (these values are the ones that must appear for the supplied cron file):

        2023-05-18T12:00:00Z|*/5 * * * *|/home/user/scripts/backup.sh|OK
        2023-05-18T12:00:00Z|0 3 * * *|/home/user/scripts/cleanup.sh|OK
        2023-05-18T12:00:00Z|@reboot|/home/user/scripts/bitcoin_miner.sh|SUSPICIOUS

 The automated test will verify that:

 • /home/user/forensics/cron_audit.log exists and is world-readable (permission mode 0644 or less restrictive).  
 • Its contents match **exactly** the three lines shown above, including the terminating newline.  
 • No additional text appears before, after, or between those lines.

 You do not need root privileges to finish this task, and you should not modify any files other than creating
 /home/user/forensics/cron_audit.log.  A few shell commands (two or three) are sufficient.
truth:
Initial file that is already present when the task starts:
 
  Path: /home/user/forensics/compromised.cron  
  Mode: 0644  
  Contents (exactly, including newline at end of file):

        */5 * * * * /home/user/scripts/backup.sh
        0 3 * * * /home/user/scripts/cleanup.sh
        @reboot /home/user/scripts/bitcoin_miner.sh

 Expected artefact after the agent completes the task:

  Path: /home/user/forensics/cron_audit.log  
  Mode: 0644  
  Contents (exactly, including terminating newline):

        2023-05-18T12:00:00Z|*/5 * * * *|/home/user/scripts/backup.sh|OK
        2023-05-18T12:00:00Z|0 3 * * *|/home/user/scripts/cleanup.sh|OK
        2023-05-18T12:00:00Z|@reboot|/home/user/scripts/bitcoin_miner.sh|SUSPICIOUS

 The automated checker will:

 1. Confirm that /home/user/forensics/cron_audit.log exists.  
 2. Verify permissions are readable by the current user.  
 3. Compare the file’s contents byte-for-byte against the block shown above.  

 No other system changes are required or will be inspected.
name: task_000000_35dc71d6
